集合论

注3.3.2 上述定义中隐含了一个假设：每当给定两个集合 $X, Y$ 和一个满足垂直线检验的性质 $P$ 时，人们可以构造一个函数对象 $f$。严格来说，函数对象 $f$ 的存在性假设应该被表述为一条明确的公理。然而，我们这里不这样做，因为事实证明这是冗余的。（更准确地说，考虑到练习3.5.10，总是可以将一个函数 $f$ 编码为一个有序三元组 $(X, Y, \{(x, f(x)) : x \in X\})$，该三元组由定义域、余域和函数的图像组成，这提供了一种使用前述集合论公理提供的运算将函数构建为对象的方法。）上述定义中还隐含了一个理解：每个函数 $f$ 自动关联一个定义域 $X$、一个余域 $Y$ 和一个定义性质 $P$。

例3.3.3 设 $X = \mathbb{N}$，$Y = \mathbb{N}$，并设 $P(x, y)$ 为性质 $y = x++$。则对于每个 $x \in \mathbb{N}$，恰好存在一个 $y \in \mathbb{N}$ 使得 $P(x, y)$ 为真，即 $y = x++$。因此我们可以定义一个与该性质关联的函数 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$，使得 $f(x) = x++$ 对所有 $x$ 成立；这就是 $\mathbb{N}$ 上的递增函数，它接受一个自然数作为输入并返回其后续数作为输出。例如 $f(4) = 5$，$f(2n + 3) = 2n + 4$，以此类推。人们可能也希望定义一个递减函数 $g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$，它与由 $y++ = x$ 定义的性质 $P(x, y)$ 关联，即 $g(x)$ 将是使 $x$ 成为其后续数的那个数。不幸的是，这并不能定义一个函数，因为当 $x = 0$ 时，不存在自然数 $y$ 使得 $y$ 的后续数等于 $x$（公理2.3）。另一方面，我们可以正当地定义一个递减函数 $h : \mathbb{N} \setminus \{0\} \to \mathbb{N}$，它与由 $y++ = x$ 定义的性质 $P(x, y)$ 关联，因为当 $x \in \mathbb{N} \setminus \{0\}$ 时，恰好存在一个自然数 $y$ 使得 $y++ = x$（引理2.2.10）。因此例如 $h(4) = 3$，$h(2n + 3) = 2n + 2$，但 $h(0)$ 未定义，因为 $0$ 不在定义域 $\mathbb{N} \setminus \{0\}$ 中。

例3.3.4（非正式）本例需要实数 $\mathbb{R}$，我们将在第5章中定义它。人们可以尝试通过将其与由 $y^2 = x$ 定义的性质 $P(x, y)$ 关联来定义平方根函数 $\sqrt{} : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$，即我们希望 $\sqrt{x}$ 是满足 $y^2 = x$ 的数 $y$。不幸的是，有两个问题阻碍了此定义真正创建一个函数。第一个是存在一些实数 $x$ 使得 $P(x, y)$ 永远不为真，例如若 $x = -1$，则不存在实数 $y$ 使得 $y^2 = x$。然而这个问题可以通过将定义域从 $\mathbb{R}$ 限制到右半轴 $[0, +\infty)$ 来解决。第二个问题是即使 $x \in [0, +\infty)$，也可能存在多于一个余域 $\mathbb{R}$ 中的 $y$ 满足 $y^2 = x$，例如若 $x = 4$，则 $y = 2$ 和 $y = -2$ 都满足性质 $P(x, y)$，即 $+2$ 和 $-2$ 都是 $4$ 的平方根。然而这个问题可以通过将余域限制到 $[0, +\infty)$ 来解决。一旦这样做，就可以使用关系 $y^2 = x$ 正确定义平方根函数 $\sqrt{} : [0, +\infty) \to [0, +\infty)$；因此 $\sqrt{x}$ 是 $[0, +\infty)$ 中满足 $y^2 = x$ 的唯一数 $y$。

一种常见的定义函数的方式是直接指定其定义域、余域以及如何从每个输入生成输出 $f(x)$；这被称为函数的显式定义。例如，例3.3.3中的函数 $f$ 可以显式定义为：$f$ 的定义域和余域均为 $\mathbb{N}$，且 $f(x) := x++$ 对所有 $x \in \mathbb{N}$ 成立。在其他情况下，我们仅通过指定将输入 $x$ 与输出 $f(x)$ 链接的性质 $P(x, y)$ 来定义函数 $f$；这是函数的隐式定义。例如，例3.3.4中的平方根函数 $\sqrt{x}$ 由关系 $(\sqrt{x})^2 = x$ 隐式定义。请注意，隐式定义仅在以下情况下有效：我们知道对于每个输入，恰好存在一个满足隐式关系的输出。在许多情况下，我们为简洁起见省略指定函数的定义域和余域，因此例如我们可以将例3.3.3中的函数 $f$ 称为"函数 $f(x) := x++$"、"函数 $x \mapsto x++$"、"函数 $x++$"，甚至极简写形式"++"。然而，过度使用这种简写可能是有危险的；有时知道函数定义域和余域是什么很重要。

我们注意到函数服从替换公理：如果 $x = x'$，则 $f(x) = f(x')$（为什么？）。换句话说，相等的输入蕴含相等的输出。另一方面，不相等的输入不一定确保不相等的输出，如下例所示：

例3.3.5 设 $X = \mathbb{N}$，$Y = \mathbb{N}$，并设 $P(x, y)$ 为性质 $y = 7$。则对于每个 $x \in \mathbb{N}$，恰好存在一个 $y$ 使得 $P(x, y)$ 为真，即数 $7$。因此我们可以创建与该性质关联的函数 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$；它就是一个常数函数，对每个输入 $x \in \mathbb{N}$ 赋予输出 $f(x) = 7$。因此不同的输入产生相同输出完全是可能的。

注3.3.6 我们现在使用括号 () 来表示数学中几种不同的事物；一方面，我们使用它们来明确运算顺序（例如比较 $2 + (3 \times 4) = 14$ 与 $(2 + 3) \times 4 = 20$），但另一方面我们也用括号来括起函数 $f(x)$ 或性质如 $P(x)$ 的参数 $x$。然而，这两种括号的用法通常从上下文来看不会产生歧义。例如，若 $a$ 是一个数，则 $a(b + c)$ 表示表达式 $a \times (b + c)$；而若 $f$ 是一个函数，则 $f(b + c)$ 表示当输入为 $b + c$ 时 $f$ 的输出。有时函数的参数用下标而不是括号来表示；例如，一个自然数序列 $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 严格来说是一个从 $\mathbb{N}$ 到 $\mathbb{N}$ 的函数，但记为 $n \mapsto a_n$ 而不是 $n \mapsto a(n)$。

注3.3.7 我们并不一定要求函数是集合，也不要求集合是函数。因此，问一个对象 $x$ 是否是函数 $f$ 的元素不一定有意义，同样将集合 $A$ 作用于输入 $x$ 以产生输出 $A(x)$ 也不一定有意义。另一方面，允许从一个函数 $f : X \to Y$ 出发构造其图像 $\{(x, f(x)) : x \in X\}$，一旦指定了定义域 $X$ 和余域 $Y$，该图像就完全描述了函数：见3.5节。

我们现在定义函数的一些基本概念和术语。第一个概念是相等性。

定义3.3.8（函数的相等性）。两个函数 $f : X \to Y$，$g : X' \to Y'$ 被称为相等的，如果它们的定义域和余域一致（即 $X = X'$ 且 $Y = Y'$），并且对所有 $x \in X$ 有 $f(x) = g(x)$。如果 $f(x)$ 和 $g(x)$ 在定义域的某些值上相等，但在其他值上不相等，则我们不认为 $f$ 和 $g$ 是相等的。如果两个函数 $f, g$ 具有不同的定义域或不同的值域，我们也不认为它们是相等的。

注3.3.9 根据这个定义，两个定义域不同或余域不同的函数，严格来说，是不同的函数。然而，在不会引起混淆的情况下，有时"滥用符号"是有用的，即如果两个函数在其公共定义域上的值一致，就将它们等同起来；这类似于软件工程中"重载"运算符的做法。见定义9.4.1之后的讨论中有一个这样的例子。

例3.3.10（非正式）函数 $x \mapsto x^2 + 2x + 1$ 和 $x \mapsto (x + 1)^2$ 在定义域 $\mathbb{R}$ 上是相等的。函数 $x \mapsto x$ 和 $x \mapsto |x|$ 在正实轴上相等，但在 $\mathbb{R}$ 上不相等；因此函数的相等性概念可能依赖于定义域的选择。

例3.3.11 一个相当平凡的函数例子是从空集到给定集合 $X$ 的空函数 $f : \emptyset \to X$。由于空集没有元素，我们不需要指定 $f$ 对任何输入的作用。尽管如此，正如空集是一个集合一样，空函数也是一个函数，尽管不是一个特别有趣的函数。注意，对于每个集合 $X$，从 $\emptyset$ 到 $X$ 只有一个函数，因为定义3.3.8断言从 $\emptyset$ 到 $X$ 的所有函数都是相等的（为什么？）。

注3.3.12 定义3.3.8与附录A.7中的相等性公理是否兼容并非一目了然，尽管练习3.3.1为此兼容性提供了证据。至少有三种方法可以处理这个问题。第一种是将定义3.3.8视为关于函数相等性的公理而非定义。第二种是提供一个更显式的函数定义，使得定义3.3.8成为一条定理；例如，可以将函数 $f : X \to Y$ 定义为一个有序三元组 $(X, Y, G)$，其中 $X$ 是定义域集合，$Y$ 是余域集合，$G = \{(x, f(x)) : x \in X\}$ 是满足垂直线检验的图像，并使用这个图像来定义对定义域中每个元素 $x$ 的函数值 $f(x) \in Y$；见练习3.5.10。第三种是从一个不包含任何函数的数学宇宙 $\mathbb{U}$ 出发，使用定义3.3.8创建该宇宙的一个更大的扩张，其中包含按定义3.3.8规定运作的函数对象。然而，这最后一种程序需要比本教材提供的更多的逻辑和模型论形式化知识，因此这里不予详述。

函数的一个基本运算是复合。

定义3.3.13（复合）。设 $f : X \to Y$ 和 $g : Y \to Z$ 是两个函数，使得 $f$ 的余域与 $g$ 的定义域是同一个集合。我们于是定义两个函数 $g$ 和 $f$ 的复合 $g \circ f : X \to Z$ 为由下式显式定义的函数

$$(g \circ f)(x) := g(f(x)).$$

如果 $f$ 的余域与 $g$ 的定义域不匹配，我们则不定义复合 $g \circ f$。

容易验证复合服从替换公理（练习3.3.1）。

例3.3.14 设 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ 是函数 $f(n) := 2n$，设 $g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ 是函数 $g(n) := n + 3$。则 $g \circ f$ 是函数

$$g \circ f(n) = g(f(n)) = g(2n) = 2n + 3,$$

因此例如 $g \circ f(1) = 5$，$g \circ f(2) = 7$，以此类推。同时，$f \circ g$ 是函数

$$f \circ g(n) = f(g(n)) = f(n + 3) = 2(n + 3) = 2n + 6,$$

因此例如 $f \circ g(1) = 8$，$f \circ g(2) = 10$，以此类推。

上述例子表明复合是不可交换的：$f \circ g$ 和 $g \circ f$ 不一定是相同的函数。然而，复合仍然是结合的：

引理3.3.15（复合是结合的）。设 $f : Z \to W$，$g : Y \to Z$，$h : X \to Y$ 是函数。则 $f \circ (g \circ h) = (f \circ g) \circ h$。

证明 由于 $g \circ h$ 是从 $X$ 到 $Z$ 的函数，$f \circ (g \circ h)$ 是从 $X$ 到 $W$ 的函数。类似地，$f \circ g$ 是从 $Y$ 到 $W$ 的函数，因此 $(f \circ g) \circ h$ 是从 $X$ 到 $W$ 的函数。因此 $f \circ (g \circ h)$ 和 $(f \circ g) \circ h$ 具有相同的定义域和余域。为了验证它们相等，我们从定义3.3.8可知需要验证对所有 $x \in X$ 有 $(f \circ (g \circ h))(x) = ((f \circ g) \circ h)(x)$。但根据定义3.3.13

$$(f \circ (g \circ h))(x) = f((g \circ h)(x))$$
$$= f(g(h(x))$$
$$= (f \circ g)(h(x))$$
$$= ((f \circ g) \circ h)(x)$$

得证。

□

注3.3.16 注意，虽然 $g$ 在表达式 $g \circ f$ 中出现在 $f$ 的左边，但函数 $g \circ f$ 首先应用最右边的函数 $f$，然后才应用 $g$。这在起初常常令人困惑；这是因为我们传统上把函数 $f$ 放在其输入 $x$ 的左边而不是右边。（在某些替代的数学记号中，函数被放在输入的右边；因此我们会写 $x \, f$ 而不是 $f(x)$，但这种记号往往被证明更容易引起混淆。）

而非澄清，至今尚未变得特别流行。）

我们现在描述某些特殊类型的函数：单射函数、满射函数和可逆函数。

定义 3.3.17（单射函数）。如果不同的元素映射到不同的元素，则函数 $f$ 是单射的（或称单射）：

$$x \neq x' \Longrightarrow f(x) \neq f(x').$$

等价的，一个函数是单射的当且仅当

$$f(x) = f(x') \Longrightarrow x = x'.$$

例 3.3.18（非正式）由 $f(n) := n^2$ 定义的函数 $f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ 不是单射的，因为不同的元素 $-1, 1$ 映射到同一个元素 $1$。另一方面，如果我们把这个函数限制到自然数上，定义函数 $g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$ 为 $g(n) := n^2$，那么 $g$ 现在是一个单射函数。因此，单射函数的概念不仅取决于函数做什么，还取决于它的定义域是什么。

注 3.3.19 如果函数 $f : X \to Y$ 不是单射的，那么可以在定义域 $X$ 中找到不同的 $x$ 和 $x'$ 使得 $f(x) = f(x')$，因此可以找到两个输入映射到一个输出。正因为如此，我们说 $f$ 是二对一而不是单射。

定义 3.3.20（满射函数）。如果 $Y$ 中的每个元素都可以通过对 $X$ 中的某个元素施加 $f$ 得到，则函数 $f$ 是满射的（或称满射）：

对于每个 $y \in Y$，存在 $x \in X$ 使得 $f(x) = y$。

例 3.3.21（非正式）由 $f(n) := n^2$ 定义的函数 $f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ 不是满射的，因为负数不在 $f$ 的像中。然而，如果我们将陪域 $\mathbb{Z}$ 限制到平方数的集合 $A := \{n^2 : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}$，那么由 $g(n) := n^2$ 定义的函数 $g : \mathbb{Z} \to A$ 现在是满射的。因此，满射函数的概念不仅取决于函数做什么，还取决于它的值域是什么。

注 3.3.22 单射性和满射性的概念在许多方面是相互对偶的；参见练习 3.3.2、3.3.4、3.3.5 中的一些证据。

定义 3.3.23（双射函数）。既是单射又是满射的函数 $f : X \to Y$ 也称为双射或可逆。

例 3.3.24 设 $f : \{0, 1, 2\} \to \{3, 4\}$ 是函数，定义为 $f(0) := 3$，$f(1) := 3$，$f(2) := 4$。这个函数不是双射的，因为如果设 $y = 3$，那么在 $\{0, 1, 2\}$ 中有多个 $x$ 使得 $f(x) = y$（这是单射性的失败）。现在设 $g : \{0, 1\} \to \{2, 3, 4\}$ 是函数，定义为 $g(0) := 2$，$g(1) := 3$；那么 $g$ 不是双射的，因为如果设 $y = 4$，则没有 $x$ 使得 $g(x) = y$（这是满射性的失败）。现在设 $h : \{0, 1, 2\} \to \{3, 4, 5\}$ 是函数，定义为 $h(0) := 3$，$h(1) := 4$，$h(2) := 5$。那么 $h$ 是双射的，因为每个元素 $3, 4, 5$ 恰好来自 $0, 1, 2$ 中的一个元素。

例 3.3.25 由 $f(n) := n++$ 定义的函数 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} \setminus \{0\}$ 是一个双射（事实上，这个事实只是重述了引理 2.2.10）。另一方面，由相同定义 $g(n) := n++$ 给出的函数 $g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ 不是双射。因此，双射函数的概念不仅取决于函数做什么，还取决于它的定义域和陪域是什么。

注 3.3.26 如果一个函数 $x \mapsto f(x)$ 是双射的，那么我们有时称 $f$ 为完美匹配或一一对应（不要与单射函数的概念混淆），并使用符号 $x \leftrightarrow f(x)$ 而不是 $x \mapsto f(x)$ 来表示 $f$ 的作用。例如，上面例子中的函数 $h$ 就是一一对应 $0 \leftrightarrow 3$，$1 \leftrightarrow 4$，$2 \leftrightarrow 5$。

注 3.3.27 一个常见的错误是说函数 $f : X \to Y$ 是双射当且仅当"对于 $X$ 中的每个 $x$，恰好有一个 $y \in Y$ 使得 $y = f(x)$"。这不是 $f$ 是双射的含义；相反，这仅仅是在说明 $f$ 是函数是什么意思。一个函数不能将一个元素映射到两个不同的元素，例如不能有一个函数 $f$ 使得 $f(0) = 1$ 且又有 $f(0) = 2$。例 3.3.25 中给出的函数 $f, g$ 不是双射，但它们仍然是函数，因为每个输入仍然恰好产生一个输出。

如果 $f$ 是双射的，那么对于每个 $y \in Y$，恰好有一个 $x$ 使得 $f(x) = y$（由于满射性至少存在一个，由于单射性至多存在一个）。这个 $x$ 的值记作 $f^{-1}(y)$；因此 $f^{-1}$ 是从 $Y$ 到 $X$ 的一个函数。我们称 $f^{-1}$ 为 $f$ 的逆函数。

练习 3.3.1 证明定义 3.3.8 中相等的定义是自反、对称和传递的。还要验证替换性质：如果 $f, \tilde{f} : X \to Y$ 和 $g, \tilde{g}: Y \to Z$ 是函数，使得 $f = \tilde{f}$ 且 $g = \tilde{g}$，那么 $g \circ f = \tilde{g} \circ \tilde{f}$。（当然，这些陈述直接从附录 A.7 中相等的公理直接应用于所讨论的函数是显然的，但这个练习的目的是表明它们也可以通过将相等的公理应用于这些函数的定义域和陪域的元素来建立，而不是应用于函数本身。）

练习 3.3.2 设 $f : X \to Y$ 和 $g: Y \to Z$ 是函数。证明如果 $f$ 和 $g$ 都是单射的，那么 $g \circ f$ 也是单射的；类似地，证明如果 $f$ 和 $g$ 都是满射的，那么 $g \circ f$ 也是满射的。

练习 3.3.3 空函数何时关于给定集合 $X$ 是单射的？满射的？双射的？

练习 3.3.4 在本节中我们给出一些复合的消去律。设 $f : X \to Y$，$\tilde{f} : X \to Y$，$g: Y \to Z$，和 $\tilde{g}: Y \to Z$ 是函数。证明如果 $g \circ f = g \circ \tilde{f}$ 且 $g$ 是单射的，那么 $f = \tilde{f}$。如果 $g$ 不是单射的，同样的陈述还成立吗？证明如果 $g \circ f = \tilde{g} \circ f$ 且 $f$ 是满射的，那么 $g = \tilde{g}$。如果 $f$ 不是满射的，同样的陈述还成立吗？

练习 3.3.5 设 $f : X \to Y$ 和 $g: Y \to Z$ 是函数。证明如果 $g \circ f$ 是单射的，那么 $f$ 必须是单射的。$g$ 也必须是单射的吗？证明如果 $g \circ f$ 是满射的，那么 $g$ 必须是满射的。$f$ 也必须是满射的吗？

练习 3.3.6 设 $f : X \to Y$ 是一个双射函数，并设 $f^{-1} : Y \to X$ 是它的逆函数。验证消去律：对于所有 $x \in X$，$f^{-1}(f(x)) = x$，且对于所有 $y \in Y$，$f(f^{-1}(y)) = y$。从而得出 $f^{-1}$ 也是可逆的，且以 $f$ 作为它的逆函数（因此 $(f^{-1})^{-1} = f$）。

Exercise 3.3.7 Let f : X \toY and g: Y \toZ be functions. Show that if f and g are bijective,

then so is g ◦f , and we have (g ◦f )−1 = f −1 ◦g−1.

Exercise 3.3.8 If X is a subset of Y, let ιX\toY : X \toY be the inclusion map from X to Y, defined

by mapping x  \tox for all x \inX, i.e., ιX\toY (x) := x for all x \inX. The map ιX\toX is in particular

called the identity map on X.

(a) Show that if X \subseteqY \subseteqZ then ιY\toZ ◦ιX\toY = ιX\toZ.

(b) Show that if f : A \toB is any function, then f = f ◦ιA\toA = ιB\toB ◦f .

(c) Show that, if f : A \toB is a bijective function, then f ◦f −1 = ιB\toB and f −1 ◦f = ιA\toA.

(d) Show that if X and Y are disjoint sets, and f : X \toZ and g: Y \toZ are functions, then there

is a unique function h : X \cupY \toZ such that h ◦ιX\toX\cupY = f and h ◦ιY\toX\cupY = g.

(e) Show that the hypothesis that X and Y are disjoint can be dropped in (d) if one adds the

additional hypothesis that f (x) = g(x) for all x \inX \capY.

Images and Inverse Images

We know that a function f : X \toY from a set X to a set Y can take individual

elements x \inX to elements f (x) \inY. Functions can also take subsets in X to

subsets in Y:

Definition 3.4.1 (Images of sets). If f : X \toY is a function from X to Y, and S is

a subset of X, we define4f (S) to be the set

f (S) := { f (x) : x \inS};

this set is a subset of Y and is sometimes called the image of S under the map f . We

sometimes call f (S) the forward image of S to distinguish it from the concept of the

inverse image f −1(S) of S, which is defined below.

Note that the set f (S) is well-defined thanks to the axiom of replacement (Axiom

3.7). One can also define f (S) using the axiom of specification (Axiom 3.6) instead

of replacement, but we leave this as an exercise to the reader. The image f (X) of

the domain is also known as the range of the function f : X \toY; it is a subset of

the codomain Y.

Example 3.4.2 If f : N \toN is the map f (x) = 2x, then the forward image of

{1, 2, 3} is {2, 4, 6}:

f ({1, 2, 3}) = {2, 4, 6}.

More informally, to compute f (S), we take every element x of S and apply f to

each element individually, and then put all the resulting objects together to form a

new set.

4 In principle this notation could collide with the existing notation f (x) for the evaluation of f at x,

if S turns out to both be a subset of X and an element of X. However, we will ignore this potential

collision as it rarely occurs in practice.

Set Theory

In the above example, the image had the same size as the original set. But sometimes the image can be smaller, because f is not one-to-one (see Definition 3.3.17):

Example 3.4.3 (Informal) Let Z be the set of integers (which we will define rigorously in the next section) and let f : Z \toZ be the map f (x) = x2, then

f ({−1, 0, 1, 2}) = {0, 1, 4}.

Note that f is not one-to-one because f (−1) = f (1).

Note that

x \inS =⇒f (x) \inf (S)

but in general

f (x) \inf (S) ⇏x \inS;

for instance in the above informal example, f (−2) lies in the set f ({−1, 0, 1, 2}),

but −2 is not in {−1, 0, 1, 2}. The correct statement is

y \inf (S) ⇐⇒y = f (x) for some x \inS

(why?).

Example 3.4.4 From Definition 3.3.20 we see that a function f : X \toY is onto if

and only if f (X) = Y.

Definition 3.4.5 (Inverse images) If U is a subset of Y, we define the set f −1(U)

to be the set

f −1(U) := {x \inX : f (x) \inU}.

In other words, f −1(U) consists of all the elements of X which map into U:

f (x) \inU ⇐⇒x \inf −1(U).

We call f −1(U) the inverse image of U.

Example 3.4.6 If f : N \toN is the map f (x) = 2x, then f ({1, 2, 3}) = {2, 4, 6},

but f −1({1, 2, 3}) = {1}. Thus the forward image of {1, 2, 3} and the backwards

image of {1, 2, 3} are quite different sets. Also note that

f ( f −1({1, 2, 3})) ̸= {1, 2, 3}

(why?).

Example 3.4.7 (Informal) If f : Z \toZ is the map f (x) = x2, then

f −1({0, 1, 4}) = {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2}.

Note that f does not have to be invertible in order for f −1(U) to make sense. Also

note that images and inverse images do not quite invert each other, for instance we

have

f −1( f ({−1, 0, 1, 2})) ̸= {−1, 0, 1, 2}

(why?).

Remark 3.4.8 If f is a bijective function, then we have defined f −1 in two slightly

different ways, but this is not an issue because the two definitions agree in this case

(Exercise 3.4.1).

As remarked earlier, functions are not necessarily sets. However, we do consider

functions to be a type of object, and in particular we should be able to consider sets

of functions. In particular, we should be able to consider the set of all functions from

a set X to a set Y. To do this we need to introduce another axiom to set theory:

Axiom 3.11 (Power set axiom). Let X and Y be sets. Then there exists a set, denoted

Y X, which consists of all the functions from X to Y, thus

f \inY X ⇐⇒( f is a function with domain X and codomain Y).

Example 3.4.9 Let X = {4, 7} and Y = {0, 1}. Then the set Y X consists of four

functions: the function that maps 4  \to0 and 7  \to0; the function that maps 4  \to0

and 7  \to1; the function that maps 4  \to1 and 7  \to0; and the function that maps

4  \to1 and 7  \to1. The reason we use the notation Y X to denote this set is that if Y

has n elements and X has m elements, then one can show that Y X has nm elements;

see Proposition 3.6.14(f).

One consequence of this axiom is

Lemma 3.4.10 Let X be a set. Then the set

{Y : Y is a subset of X}

is a set. That is to say, there exists a set Z such that

Y \inZ ⇐⇒Y \subseteqX

for all objects Y.

Proof See Exercise 3.4.6.

□

Remark 3.4.11 The set {Y : Y is a subset of X} is known as the power set of X and

is denoted 2X. For instance, if a, b, c are distinct objects, we have

2{a,b,c} = {\emptyset, {a}, {b}, {c}, {a, b}, {a, c}, {b, c}, {a, b, c}}.

Note that while {a, b, c} has 3 elements, 2{a,b,c} has 23 = 8 elements. This gives a

hint as to why we refer to the power set of X as 2X; we return to this issue in Chap. 8.

Set Theory

For sake of completeness, let us now add one further axiom to our set theory,

in which we enhance the axiom of pairwise union to allow unions of much larger

collections of sets.

Axiom 3.12 (Union). Let A be a set, all of whose elements are themselves sets.

Then there exists a set  A whose elements are precisely those objects which are

elements of the elements of A, thus for all objects x

Example 3.4.12 If A = {{2, 3}, {3, 4}, {4, 5}}, then  A = {2, 3, 4, 5} (why?).

The axiom of union, combined with the axiom of pair set, implies the axiom of

pairwise union (Exercise 3.4.8). Another important consequence of this axiom is that

if one has some set I, and for every element α \inI we have some set Aα, then we

(compare with (3.2)).

Remark 3.4.13 The axioms of set theory that we have introduced (Axioms 3.1

and 3.12, excluding the dangerous Axiom 3.9) are known as the Zermelo–Fraenkel

axioms of set theory,5 after Ernest Zermelo (1871–1953) and Abraham Fraenkel

(1891–1965). There is one further axiom we will eventually need, the famous axiom

of choice (see Sect. 8.4), giving rise to the Zermelo–Fraenkel–Choice (ZFC) axioms

of set theory, but we will not need this axiom for some time.

Exercise 3.4.1 Let f : X \toY be a bijective function, and let f −1 : Y \toX be its inverse. Let V

be any subset of Y. Prove that the forward image of V under f −1 is the same set as the inverse image

of V under f ; thus the fact that both sets are denoted by f −1(V ) will not lead to any inconsistency.

Exercise 3.4.2 Let f : X \toY be a function from one set X to another set Y, let S be a subset of

X, and let U be a subset of Y.

(i) What, in general, can one say about f −1( f (S)) and S?

(ii) What about f ( f −1(U)) and U?

(iii) What about f −1( f ( f −1(U))) and f −1(U)?

Exercise 3.4.3 Let A, B be two subsets of a set X, and let f : X \toY be a function. Show that

f (A \capB) \subseteqf (A) \capf (B), that f (A)\ f (B) \subseteqf (A\B), f (A \cupB) = f (A) \cupf (B). For the first

two statements, is it true that the \subseteqrelation can be improved to =?

Exercise 3.4.4 Let f : X \toY be a function from one set X to another set Y, and letU, V be subsets

of Y. Show that f −1(U \cupV ) = f −1(U) \cupf −1(V ), that f −1(U \capV ) = f −1(U) \capf −1(V ), and

that f −1(U\V ) = f −1(U)\ f −1(V ).

Exercise 3.4.5 Let f : X \toY be a function from one set X to another set Y. Show that

f ( f −1(S)) = S for every S \subseteqY if and only if f is surjective. Show that f −1( f (S)) = S for

every S \subseteqX if and only if f is injective.

Exercise 3.4.6

(i) Prove Lemma 3.4.10. (Hint: start with the set {0, 1}X and apply the replacement axiom, replacing each function f with the object f −1({1}).) See also Exercise 3.5.11.

(ii) Conversely, show that Axiom 3.11 can be deduced the preceding axioms of set theory if one

accepts Lemma 3.4.10 as an axiom. (This may help explain why we refer to Axiom 3.11 as

the “power set axiom”.)

Exercise 3.4.7 Let X, Y be sets. Define a partial function from X to Y to be any function f : X′ \to

Y ′ whose domain X′ is a subset of X, and whose codomain Y ′ is a subset of Y. Show that the

collection of all partial functions from X to Y is itself a set. (Hint: use Exercise 3.4.6, the power set

axiom, the replacement axiom, and the union axiom.)

Exercise 3.4.8 Show that Axiom 3.5 can be deduced from Axiom 3.1, Axiom 3.4, and Axiom 3.12.

Exercise 3.4.9 Show that if β and β′ are two elements of a set I, and to each α \inI we assign a set

Aα, then

and so the definition of 

true.

shown to be equivalent to each other.

This should be compared with De Morgan’s laws in Proposition 3.1.27 (although one cannot derive

the above identities directly from De Morgan’s laws, as I could be infinite).

3.5

Cartesian Products

In addition to the basic operations of union, intersection, and differencing, another

fundamental operation on sets is that of the Cartesian product. To define this notion,

we first need the concept of an ordered pair.

Definition 3.5.1 (Ordered pair). If x and y are any objects (possibly equal), we

define the ordered pair (x, y) to be a new object, consisting of x as its first component

and y as its second component. Two ordered pairs (x, y) and (x′, y′) are considered

equal if and only if both their components match, i.e.,

(x, y) = (x′, y′) ⇐⇒(x = x′ and y = y′).

(3.5)

This notion of equality is consistent with the usual axioms of equality (Exercise

3.5.3). Thus for instance, the pair (3, 5) is equal to the pair (2 + 1, 3 + 2), but is

distinct from the pairs (5, 3), (3, 3), and (2, 5). (This is in contrast to sets, where

{3, 5} and {5, 3} are equal.)

Remark 3.5.2 Strictly speaking, this definition is partly an axiom, because we have

simply postulated that given any two objects x and y, that an object of the form (x, y)

exists. However, it is possible to define an ordered pair using the axioms of set theory

in such a way that we do not need any further postulates (see Exercise 3.5.1).

Remark 3.5.3 We have now “overloaded” the parenthesis symbols () once again;

they now are not only used to denote grouping of operators and arguments of functions, but also to enclose ordered pairs. This is usually not a problem in practice as

one can still determine what usage the symbols () were intended for from context.

Definition 3.5.4 (Cartesian product). If X and Y are sets, then we define the Cartesian product X \times Y to be the collection of ordered pairs, whose first component lies

in X and second component lies in Y, thus
sections.content_zh:
练习 3.3.7 设 $f : X \to Y$ 和 $g: Y \to Z$ 为函数。证明若 $f$ 和 $g$ 都是双射，则 $g \circ f$ 也是双射，且有 $(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$。

练习 3.3.8 若 $X$ 是 $Y$ 的子集，记包含映射 $\iota_{X \to Y} : X \to Y$ 为对所有 $x \in X$ 将 $x$ 映射到 $x$ 的函数，即 $\iota_{X \to Y}(x) := x$ 对所有 $x \in X$ 成立。特别地，映射 $\iota_{X \to X}$ 称为 $X$ 上的恒等映射。

(a) 证明若 $X \subseteq Y \subseteq Z$，则 $\iota_{Y \to Z} \circ \iota_{X \to Y} = \iota_{X \to Z}$。

(b) 证明若 $f : A \to B$ 是任意函数，则 $f = f \circ \iota_{A \to A} = \iota_{B \to B} \circ f$。

(c) 证明若 $f : A \to B$ 是双射函数，则 $f \circ f^{-1} = \iota_{B \to B}$ 且 $f^{-1} \circ f = \iota_{A \to A}$。

(d) 证明若 $X$ 和 $Y$ 是不相交集合，$f : X \to Z$ 和 $g : Y \to Z$ 是函数，则存在唯一的函数 $h : X \cup Y \to Z$ 使得 $h \circ \iota_{X \to X \cup Y} = f$ 且 $h \circ \iota_{Y \to X \cup Y} = g$。

(e) 证明若在 (d) 中添加额外的假设 $f(x) = g(x)$ 对所有 $x \in X \cap Y$ 成立，则可去掉 $X$ 和 $Y$ 不相交的条件。

像与逆像

我们知道，从集合 $X$ 到集合 $Y$ 的函数 $f : X \to Y$ 可以将单个元素 $x \in X$ 映射到元素 $f(x) \in Y$。函数也可以将 $X$ 中的子集映射到 $Y$ 中的子集：

定义 3.4.1（集合的像）。设 $f : X \to Y$ 是从 $X$ 到 $Y$ 的函数，$S$ 是 $X$ 的子集，定义⁴ $f(S)$ 为集合

$$f(S) := \{ f(x) : x \in S\};$$

该集合是 $Y$ 的子集，有时称为 $S$ 在映射 $f$ 下的像。有时为了与下定义的逆像 $f^{-1}(S)$ 区分，我们称 $f(S)$ 为前向像。

注意，根据替换公理（公理 3.7），集合 $f(S)$ 是良好定义的。也可以用指定公理（公理 3.6）来定义 $f(S)$，但我们将其留给读者作为练习。定义域 $X$ 的像 $f(X)$ 也称为函数 $f : X \to Y$ 的值域；它是陪域 $Y$ 的子集。

例 3.4.2 若 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ 是映射 $f(x) = 2x$，则 $\{1, 2, 3\}$ 的前向像是 $\{2, 4, 6\}$：

$$f(\{1, 2, 3\}) = \{2, 4, 6\}.$$

更通俗地说，要计算 $f(S)$，我们取 $S$ 的每个元素 $x$，对每个元素分别应用 $f$，然后将所有结果对象放在一起形成一个新的集合。

⁴ 原则上这个记号可能与在 $x$ 处求值 $f$ 的已有记号 $f(x)$ 冲突，如果 $S$ 恰好既是 $X$ 的子集又是 $X$ 的元素的话。然而，我们将忽略这种潜在的冲突，因为在实践中很少发生。

集合论

在上述例子中，像与原集合有相同的大小。但有时像可能更小，因为 $f$ 不是单射的（见定义 3.3.17）：

例 3.4.3（非正式）。设 $Z$ 为整数集（我们将在下一节严格定义），设 $f : Z \to Z$ 是映射 $f(x) = x^2$，则

$$f(\{-1, 0, 1, 2\}) = \{0, 1, 4\}.$$

注意 $f$ 不是单射的，因为 $f(-1) = f(1)$。

注意

$$x \in S \Longrightarrow f(x) \in f(S)$$

但一般来说

$$f(x) \in f(S) \nRightarrow x \in S;$$

例如在上述非正式例子中，$f(-2)$ 属于集合 $f(\{-1, 0, 1, 2\})$，但 $-2$ 不在 $\{-1, 0, 1, 2\}$ 中。正确的陈述是

$$y \in f(S) \Longleftrightarrow y = f(x) \text{ 对某个 } x \in S$$

（为什么？）。

例 3.4.4 由定义 3.3.20 可知，函数 $f : X \to Y$ 是满射当且仅当 $f(X) = Y$。

定义 3.4.5（逆像）。若 $U$ 是 $Y$ 的子集，定义集合 $f^{-1}(U)$ 为

$$f^{-1}(U) := \{x \in X : f(x) \in U\}.$$

换句话说，$f^{-1}(U)$ 由所有映射到 $U$ 中的 $X$ 的元素组成：

$$f(x) \in U \Longleftrightarrow x \in f^{-1}(U).$$

我们称 $f^{-1}(U)$ 为 $U$ 的逆像。

例 3.4.6 若 $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ 是映射 $f(x) = 2x$，则 $f(\{1, 2, 3\}) = \{2, 4, 6\}$，但 $f^{-1}(\{1, 2, 3\}) = \{1\}$。因此 $\{1, 2, 3\}$ 的前向像和后向像是完全不同的集合。还要注意

$$f(f^{-1}(\{1, 2, 3\})) \neq \{1, 2, 3\}$$

（为什么？）。

例 3.4.7（非正式）。若 $f : Z \to Z$ 是映射 $f(x) = x^2$，则

$$f^{-1}(\{0, 1, 4\}) = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}.$$

注意 $f$ 不必是可逆的，$f^{-1}(U)$ 才有意义。还要注意像和逆像并不完全互逆，例如我们有

$$f^{-1}(f(\{-1, 0, 1, 2\})) \neq \{-1, 0, 1, 2\}$$

（为什么？）。

注 3.4.8 若 $f$ 是双射函数，则我们以两种略有不同的方式定义了 $f^{-1}$，但这不是问题，因为这两种定义在这种情况下是一致的（练习 3.4.1）。

如前所述，函数不一定是集合。然而，我们确实认为函数是一种对象，特别是我们应该能够考虑函数的集合。特别地，我们应该能够考虑从集合 $X$ 到集合 $Y$ 的所有函数的集合。为此，我们需要向集合论引入另一条公理：

公理 3.11（幂集公理）。设 $X$ 和 $Y$ 是集合。则存在一个集合，记为 $Y^X$，它由所有从 $X$ 到 $Y$ 的函数组成，因此

$$f \in Y^X \Longleftrightarrow (f \text{ 是定义域为 } X \text{ 且陪域为 } Y \text{ 的函数}).$$

例 3.4.9 设 $X = \{4, 7\}$ 和 $Y = \{0, 1\}$。则集合 $Y^X$ 包含四个函数：映射 $4 \mapsto 0$ 且 $7 \mapsto 0$ 的函数；映射 $4 \mapsto 0$ 且 $7 \mapsto 1$ 的函数；映射 $4 \mapsto 1$ 且 $7 \mapsto 0$ 的函数；以及映射 $4 \mapsto 1$ 且 $7 \mapsto 1$ 的函数。我们用记号 $Y^X$ 来表示这个集合的原因是，如果 $Y$ 有 $n$ 个元素且 $X$ 有 $m$ 个元素，则可以证明 $Y^X$ 有 $n^m$ 个元素；见命题 3.6.14(f)。

这条公理的一个推论是

引理 3.4.10 设 $X$ 是一个集合。则集合

$$\{Y : Y \text{ 是 } X \text{ 的子集}\}$$

是一个集合。换句话说，存在一个集合 $Z$ 使得对所有对象 $Y$，

$$Y \in Z \Longleftrightarrow Y \subseteq X.$$

证明 见练习 3.4.6。

□

注 3.4.11 集合 $\{Y : Y \text{ 是 } X \text{ 的子集}\}$ 称为 $X$ 的幂集，记为 $2^X$。例如，若 $a, b, c$ 是互不相同的对象，我们有

$$2^{\{a,b,c\}} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{c\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, c\}, \{b, c\}, \{a, b, c\}\}.$$

注意 $\{a, b, c\}$ 有 3 个元素，而 $2^{\{a,b,c\}}$ 有 $2^3 = 8$ 个元素。这给了我们一个线索，说明为什么我们将 $X$ 的幂集记为 $2^X$；我们将在第 8 章回到这个问题。

集合论

为了完整起见，让我们现在向集合论添加一条进一步的公理，在这条公理中，我们将成对并集公理推广到允许更大量集合的并集。

公理 3.12（并集）。设 $A$ 是一个集合，其所有元素本身也是集合。则存在一个集合 $\bigcup A$，其元素恰好是那些作为 $A$ 中元素的元素的对象，因此对所有对象 $x$，

$$x \in \bigcup A \Longleftrightarrow (x \in S \text{ 对某个 } S \in A).$$

例 3.4.12 若 $A = \{\{2, 3\}, \{3, 4\}, \{4, 5\}\}$，则 $\bigcup A = \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$（为什么？）。

并集公理与配对公理一起可以推出成对并集公理（练习 3.4.8）。这条公理的另一个重要推论是，如果有一个集合 $I$，且对每个 $\alpha \in I$ 我们都有一个集合 $A_\alpha$，那么我们可以形成并集集合 $\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha$，定义为

$$\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha := \bigcup \{A_\alpha : \alpha \in I\},$$

这根据替换公理和并集公理是一个集合。例如，若 $I = \{1, 2, 3\}$，$A_1 := \{2, 3\}$，$A_2 := \{3, 4\}$，$A_3 := \{4, 5\}$，则 $\bigcup_{\alpha \in \{1,2,3\}} A_\alpha = \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$。更一般地，对任何对象 $y$，

$$y \in \bigcup_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha \Longleftrightarrow (y \in A_\alpha \text{ 对某个 } \alpha \in I). \tag{3.2}$$

在这种情形中，我们通常称 $I$ 为指标集，其元素 $\alpha$ 称为标签；集合 $A_\alpha$ 称为一族集合，并按标签 $\alpha \in I$ 索引。注意如果 $I$ 是空集，则 $\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha$ 自动也是空集（为什么？）。

类似地，我们可以形成一族集合的交集，只要指标集非空。更具体地说，给定任意非空集合 $I$，并给定对每个 $\alpha \in I$ 指派一个集合 $A_\alpha$，我们可以通过先选择 $I$ 的某个元素 $\beta$（由于 $I$ 非空，我们可以做到这一点）来定义交集 $\bigcap_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha$，设

$$\bigcap_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha := \{x \in A_\beta : x \in A_\alpha \text{ 对所有 } \alpha \in I\}, \tag{3.3}$$

这根据指定公理是一个集合。这个定义可能看起来依赖于 $\beta$ 的选择，但实际上并不依赖（练习 3.4.9）。注意对任何对象 $y$，

$$y \in \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha \Longleftrightarrow (y \in A_\alpha \text{ 对所有 } \alpha \in I) \tag{3.4}$$

（与 (3.2) 比较）。

注 3.4.13 我们引入的集合论公理（公理 3.1 至 3.12，排除有问题的公理 3.9）被称为集合论的 Zermelo–Fraenkel 公理⁵，以恩斯特·策梅洛（1871—1953）和阿布拉罕·弗兰克尔（1891—1965）命名。还有一条我们最终需要的公理，即著名的选择公理（见 8.4 节），这给出了集合论的 Zermelo–Fraenkel–Choice（ZFC）公理，但我们暂时不需要这条公理。

练习 3.4.1 设 $f : X \to Y$ 是双射函数，$f^{-1} : Y \to X$ 是其逆函数。设 $V$ 是 $Y$ 的任意子集。证明 $f^{-1}$ 在 $V$ 上的前向像与 $f$ 在 $V$ 上的逆像是同一个集合；因此这两个集合都用 $f^{-1}(V)$ 表示不会导致任何不一致。

练习 3.4.2 设 $f : X \to Y$ 是从集合 $X$ 到集合 $Y$ 的函数，$S$ 是 $X$ 的子集，$U$ 是 $Y$ 的子集。

(i) 一般来说，$f^{-1}(f(S))$ 和 $S$ 之间能说什么？

(ii) $f(f^{-1}(U))$ 和 $U$ 呢？

(iii) $f^{-1}(f(f^{-1}(U)))$ 和 $f^{-1}(U)$ 呢？

练习 3.4.3 设 $A, B$ 是集合 $X$ 的两个子集，$f : X \to Y$ 是函数。证明 $f(A \cap B) \subseteq f(A) \cap f(B)$，$f(A) \setminus f(B) \subseteq f(A \setminus B)$，$f(A \cup B) = f(A) \cup f(B)$。对于前两个命题，关系 $\subseteq$ 能否改进为 $=$？

练习 3.4.4 设 $f : X \to Y$ 是从集合 $X$ 到集合 $Y$ 的函数，$U, V$ 是 $Y$ 的子集。证明 $f^{-1}(U \cup V) = f^{-1}(U) \cup f^{-1}(V)$，$f^{-1}(U \cap V) = f^{-1}(U) \cap f^{-1}(V)$，以及 $f^{-1}(U \setminus V) = f^{-1}(U) \setminus f^{-1}(V)$。

练习 3.4.5 设 $f : X \to Y$ 是从集合 $X$ 到集合 $Y$ 的函数。证明 $f(f^{-1}(S)) = S$ 对每个 $S \subseteq Y$ 成立当且仅当 $f$ 是满射；证明 $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ 对每个 $S \subseteq X$ 成立当且仅当 $f$ 是单射。

练习 3.4.6

(i) 证明引理 3.4.10。（提示：从集合 $\{0, 1\}^X$ 开始，应用替换公理，将每个函数 $f$ 替换为对象 $f^{-1}(\{1\})$。）另见练习 3.5.11。

(ii) 反过来，证明如果接受引理 3.4.10 作为公理，则可以从集合论的前述公理推导出公理 3.11。（这有助于解释为什么我们将公理 3.11 称为"幂集公理"。）

练习 3.4.7 设 $X, Y$ 是集合。定义从 $X$ 到 $Y$ 的偏函数为定义域 $X'$ 是 $X$ 的子集且陪域 $Y'$ 是 $Y$ 的子集的任意函数 $f : X' \to Y'$。证明所有从 $X$ 到 $Y$ 的偏函数的全体本身是一个集合。（提示：使用练习

sections.content_en:
X \times Y = {(x, y) : x \inX, y \inY}

or equivalently

a \in(X \times Y) ⇐⇒(a = (x, y) for some x \inX and y \inY).

One can show that the Cartesian product X \times Y is in fact a set; see Exercise 3.5.1.

Example 3.5.5 If X := {1, 2} and Y := {3, 4, 5}, then

X \times Y = {(1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5)}

and

Y \times X = {(3, 1), (4, 1), (5, 1), (3, 2), (4, 2), (5, 2)}.

Thus, strictly speaking, X \times Y and Y \times X are different sets, although they are very

similar. For instance, they always have the same number of elements (Exercise 3.6.5).

Let f : X \times Y \toZ be a function whose domain X \times Y is a Cartesian product of

two other sets X and Y. Then f can either be thought of as a function of one variable,

mapping the single input of an ordered pair (x, y) in X \times Y to an output6f (x, y) in

Z, or as a function of two variables, mapping an input x \inX and another input y \inY

to a single output f (x, y) in Z. While the two notions are technically different, we

will not bother to distinguish the two, and think of f simultaneously as a function of

one variable with domain X \times Y and as a function of two variables with domains X

and Y. Thus for instance the addition operation + on the natural numbers can now

be re-interpreted as a function +: N \times N \toN, defined by (x, y)  \tox + y.

Once one has the notion of an ordered pair, one can also define an ordered triple

(x, y, z) of three objects (x, y, z) by the formula (x, y, z) := ((x, y), z). One could

continue in this fashion and define ordered quadruples, etc., but we shall instead use

a different construction to build ordered n-tuples:

Definition 3.5.6 (Ordered n-tuple and n-fold Cartesian product). Let n be a natural

number. An ordered n-tuple (xi)1\leqi\leqn (also denoted (x1, ... , xn)) is a collection of

objects xi, one for every natural number i between 1 and n; we refer to xi as the

ith component of the ordered n-tuple. Two ordered n-tuples (xi)1\leqi\leqn and (yi)1\leqi\leqn

are said to be equal iff xi = yi for all 1 \leqi \leqn. If (Xi)1\leqi\leqn is an ordered ntuple of sets, we define their Cartesian product 

1\leqi\leqn Xi (also denoted n

i=1 Xi or

X1 \times ... \times Xn) by

 

1\leqi\leqn

Xi := {(xi)1\leqi\leqn : xi \inXi for all 1 \leqi \leqn}.

6 Here (and in the rest of this text) we adopt the very common practice of abbreviating f ((x, y)) as

f (x, y).

Set Theory

Again, this definition simply postulates that an ordered n-tuple and a Cartesian

product always exist when needed, but using the axioms of set theory one can explicitly construct these objects; see Exercise 3.5.2.

Remark 3.5.7 One can generalize this construction to infinite Cartesian products;

see Definition 8.4.1.

Example 3.5.8 Let a1, b1, a2, b2, a3, b3 be objects, and let X1 := {a1, b1}, X2 :=

{a2, b2}, and X3 := {a3, b3}. Then we have

X1 \times X2 \times X3 = {(a1, a2, a3), (a1, a2, b3), (a1, b2, a3), (a1, b2, b3),

(b1, a2, a3), (b1, a2, b3), (b1, b2, a3), (b1, b2, b3)}

(X1 \times X2) \times X3 = {((a1, a2), a3), ((a1, a2), b3), ((a1, b2), a3), ((a1, b2), b3),

((b1, a2), a3), ((b1, a2), b3), ((b1, b2), a3), ((b1, b2), b3)}

X1 \times (X2 \times X3) = {(a1, (a2, a3)), (a1, (a2, b3)), (a1, (b2, a3)), (a1, (b2, b3)),

(b1, (a2, a3)), (b1, (a2, b3)), (b1, (b2, a3)), (b1, (b2, b3))}.

Thus, strictly speaking, the sets X1 \times X2 \times X3, (X1 \times X2) \times X3, and X1 \times (X2 \times

X3) are distinct. However, they are clearly very related to each other (for instance,

there are obvious bijections between any two of the three sets), and it is common

in practice to neglect the minor distinctions between these sets and pretend that

they are in fact equal. Thus a function f : X1 \times X2 \times X3 \toY can be thought of

as a function of one variable (x1, x2, x3) \inX1 \times X2 \times X3, or as a function of three

variables x1 \inX1, x2 \inX2, x3 \inX3, or as a function of two variables x1 \inX1,

(x2, x3) \inX2 \times X3, and so forth; we will not bother to distinguish between these

different perspectives.

Remark 3.5.9 An ordered n-tuple (x1, ... , xn) of objects is also called an ordered

sequence of n elements, or a finite sequence for short. In Chap. 5 we shall also

introduce the very useful concept of an infinite sequence.

Example 3.5.10 If x is an object, then (x) is a 1-tuple, which we shall identify with

x itself (even though the two are, strictly speaking, not the same object). Then if X1

is any set, then the Cartesian product 

1\leqi\leq1 Xi is just X1 (why?). Also, the empty

Cartesian product 

1\leqi\leq0 Xi gives, not the empty set {}, but rather the singleton set

{()} whose only element is the 0-tuple (), also known as the empty tuple.

If n is a natural number, we often write Xn as shorthand for the n-fold Cartesian

product Xn := 

1\leqi\leqn X. Thus X1 is essentially the same set as X (if we ignore

the distinction between an object x and the 1-tuple (x)), while X2 is essentially the

Cartesian product X \times X. The set X0 is a singleton set {()} (why?).

We can now generalize the single choice lemma (Lemma 3.1.5) to allow for

multiple (but finite) number of choices.

Lemma 3.5.11 (Finite choice). Let n \geq1 be a natural number, and for each natural

number 1 \leqi \leqn, let Xi be a non-empty set. Then there exists an n-tuple (xi)1\leqi\leqn

such that xi \inXi for all 1 \leqi \leqn. In other words, if each Xi is non-empty, then the

set 

1\leqi\leqn Xi is also non-empty.

Proof We induct on n (starting with the base case n = 1; the claim is also vacuously

true with n = 0 but is not particularly interesting in that case). When n = 1 the

claim follows from Lemma 3.1.5 (why?). Now suppose inductively that the claim

has already been proven for some n; we will now prove it for n++. Let X1, ... , Xn++

be a collection of non-empty sets. By induction hypothesis, we can find an n-tuple

(xi)1\leqi\leqn such that xi \inXi for all 1 \leqi \leqn. Also, since Xn++ is non-empty, by

Lemma 3.1.5 we may find an object a such that a \inXn++. If we thus define the n++-

tuple (yi)1\leqi\leqn++ by setting yi := xi when 1 \leqi \leqn and yi := a when i = n++ it

is clear that yi \inXi for all 1 \leqi \leqn++, thus closing the induction.

□

Remark 3.5.12 It is intuitively plausible that this lemma should be extended to allow

for an infinite number of choices, but this cannot be done automatically; it requires

an additional axiom, the axiom of choice. See Section 8.4.

Exercise 3.5.1

(i) Suppose we define the ordered pair (x, y) for any objects x and y by the formula

(x, y) := {{x}, {x, y}} (thus using several applications of Axiom 3.4). Thus for instance (1, 2)

is the set {{1}, {1, 2}}, (2, 1) is the set {{2}, {2, 1}}, and (1, 1) is the set {{1}}. Show that such a

definition (known as the Kuratowski definition of an ordered pair) indeed obeys the property

(3.5).

(ii) Suppose we instead define an ordered pair using the alternate definition (x, y) := {x, {x, y}}.

Show that this definition (known as the short definition of an ordered pair) also verifies (3.5)

and is thus also an acceptable definition of ordered pair. (Warning: this is tricky; one needs the

axiom of regularity, and in particular Exercise 3.2.2.)

(iii) Show that regardless of the definition of ordered pair, the Cartesian product X \times Y of any two

sets X, Y is again a set. (Hint: first use the axiom of replacement to show that for any x \inX,

that {(x, y) : y \inY} is a set, and then apply the axiom of union.)

Exercise 3.5.2 Suppose we define7 an ordered n-tuple to be a surjective function x : {i \inN : 1 \leq

i \leqn} \toX whose codomain is some arbitrary set X (so different ordered n-tuples are allowed to

have different ranges); we then write xi for x(i) and also write x as (xi)1\leqi\leqn. Using this definition,

verify that we have (xi)1\leqi\leqn = (yi)1\leqi\leqn if and only if xi = yi for all 1 \leqi \leqn. Also, show that

if (Xi)1\leqi\leqn are an ordered n-tuple of sets, then the Cartesian product, as defined in Definition

3.5.6, is indeed a set. (Hint: use Exercise 3.4.7 and the axiom of specification.)

Exercise 3.5.3 Show that the definitions of equality for ordered pair and ordered n-tuple are consistent with the reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity axioms, in the sense that if these axioms are

assumed to hold for the individual components x, y of an ordered pair (x, y), then they hold for the

ordered pair itself.

Exercise 3.5.4 Let A, B, C be sets. Show that A \times (B \cupC) = (A \times B) \cup(A \times C), that A \times (B \cap

C) = (A \times B) \cap(A \times C), and that A \times (B\C) = (A \times B)\(A \times C). (One can of course prove

similar identities in which the rôles of the left and right factors of the Cartesian product are reversed.)

7 Technically,thisconstructionoforderedn-tuple isnot compatible withthe constructionsofordered

pairs in Exercise 3.5.1, but this does not cause a difficulty in practice; for instance, one can use the

definition of an ordered 2-tuple here to replace the construction in Exercise 3.5.1, or one can make

a rather pedantic distinction between an ordered 2-tuple and an ordered pair in one’s mathematical

arguments.

Set Theory

Exercise 3.5.5 Let A, B, C, D be sets. Show that (A \times B) \cap(C \times D) = (A \capC) \times (B \capD). Is it

true that (A \times B) \cup(C \times D) = (A \cupC) \times (B \cupD)? Is it true that (A \times B)\(C \times D) = (A\C) \times

(B\D)?

Exercise 3.5.6 Let A, B, C, D be non-empty sets. Show that A \times B \subseteqC \times D if and only if A \subseteqC

and B \subseteqD, and that A \times B = C \times D if and only if A = C and B = D. What happens if some or

all of the hypotheses that the A, B, C, D are non-empty are removed?

Exercise 3.5.7 Let X, Y be sets, and let πX\timesY\toX : X \times Y \toX and πX\timesY\toY : X \times Y \toY be

the maps πX\timesY\toX(x, y) := x and πX\timesY\toY (x, y) := y; these maps are known as the co-ordinate

functions on X \times Y. Show that for any functions f : Z \toX and g: Z \toY, there exists a unique

function h : Z \toX \times Y such that πX\timesY\toX ◦h = f and πX\timesY\toY ◦h = g. (Compare this to the

last part of Exercise 3.3.8, and to Exercise 3.1.7.) This function h is known as the pairing of f and

g and is denoted h = ( f, g).

Exercise 3.5.8 Let X1, ... , Xn be sets. Show that the Cartesian product n

i=1 Xi is empty if and

only if at least one of the Xi is empty.

Exercise 3.5.9 Suppose that I and J are two sets, and for all α \inI let Aα be a set, and for all β \inJ

let Bβ be a set. Show that (

α\inI Aα) \cap(

β\inJ Bβ) = 

(α,β)\inI\timesJ(Aα \capBβ). What happens if one

interchanges all the union and intersection symbols here?

Exercise 3.5.10 If f : X \toY is a function, define the graph of f to be the subset of X \times Y defined

by {(x, f (x)) : x \inX}.

(i) Show that two functions f : X \toY, ˜f : X \toY are equal if and only if they have the same

graph.

(ii) Conversely, if G is any subset of X \times Y with the property that for each x \inX, the set {y \inY :

(x, y) \inG} has exactly one element (or in other words, G obeys the vertical line test), show

that there is exactly one function f : X \toY whose graph is equal to G.

(iii) Suppose we define8a function f to be an ordered triple f = (X, Y, G), where X, Y are sets,

and G is a subset of X \times Y that obeys the vertical line test. We then define the domain of such

a triple to be X, the codomain to be Y and for every x \inX, we define f (x) to be the unique

y \inY such that (x, y) \inG. Show that this definition is compatible with Definition 3.3.1 in the

sense that every choice of domain X, codomain Y, and property P(x, y) obeying the vertical

line test produces a function as defined here that obeys all the properties required of it in that

definition, and is also similarly compatible with Definition 3.3.8.

Exercise 3.5.11 Show that Axiom 3.11 can in fact be deduced from Lemma 3.4.10 and the other

axioms of set theory, and thus Lemma 3.4.10 can be used as an alternate formulation of the power

set axiom. (Hint: for any two sets X and Y, use Lemma 3.4.10 and the axiom of specification to

construct the set of all subsets of X \times Y which obey the vertical line test. Then use Exercise 3.5.10

and the axiom of replacement.)

Exercise 3.5.12 This exercise will establish a rigorous version of Proposition 2.1.16 that avoids

circularity (in particular, avoiding the use of any object that required Proposition 2.1.16 to construct).

(i) Let X be a set, let f : N \times X \toX be a function, and let c be an element of X. Show that there

exists a function a : X \toX such that

a(0) = c

and

a(n++) = f (n, a(n)) for all n \inN,

8 Note that this definition is not circular, because the notion of a function was not used to define

$X \times Y = \{(x, y) : x \in X, y \in Y\}$

或者等价地

$a \in (X \times Y) \Leftrightarrow (a = (x, y)$ 对于某些 $x \in X$ 和 $y \in Y$ 成立)。

可以证明笛卡尔积 $X \times Y$ 实际上是一个集合；参见习题 3.5.1。

**例 3.5.5** 设 $X := \{1, 2\}$ 和 $Y := \{3, 4, 5\}$，则

$X \times Y = \{(1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5)\}$

且

$Y \times X = \{(3, 1), (4, 1), (5, 1), (3, 2), (4, 2), (5, 2)\}$。

因此，严格来说，$X \times Y$ 和 $Y \times X$ 是不同的集合，尽管它们非常相似。例如，它们的元素个数总是相同的（参见习题 3.6.5）。

设 $f : X \times Y \to Z$ 是一个函数，其定义域 $X \times Y$ 是另外两个集合 $X$ 和 $Y$ 的笛卡尔积。则 $f$ 可以被看作是一个单变量函数，将 $X \times Y$ 中的一个有序对 $(x, y)$ 的单一输入映射到 $Z$ 中的一个输出 $^6 f(x, y)$，或者被看作是一个双变量函数，将一个输入 $x \in X$ 和另一个输入 $y \in Y$ 映射到 $Z$ 中的单一输出 $f(x, y)$。虽然这两种概念技术上有所不同，但我们将不区分这两者，而是同时将 $f$ 看作是一个定义域为 $X \times Y$ 的单变量函数，以及定义域分别为 $X$ 和 $Y$ 的双变量函数。例如，自然数上的加法运算 $+$ 现在可以重新解释为一个函数 $+ : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$，定义为 $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$。

一旦有了有序对的概念，我们也可以通过公式 $(x, y, z) := ((x, y), z)$ 来定义三个对象 $(x, y, z)$ 的有序三元组。我们可以继续这种方式定义有序四元组等，但我们将使用另一种构造来建立有序 $n$ 元组：

**定义 3.5.6**（有序 $n$ 元组和 $n$ 重笛卡尔积）。设 $n$ 是一个自然数。一个有序 $n$ 元组 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$（也记作 $(x_1, ..., x_n)$）是对象 $x_i$ 的一个收集，其中 $i$ 取遍 1 到 $n$ 之间的每个自然数；我们称 $x_i$ 为该有序 $n$ 元组的第 $i$ 个分量。两个有序 $n$ 元组 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 和 $(y_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 相等当且仅当对所有 $1 \leq i \leq n$ 有 $x_i = y_i$。如果 $(X_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 是一组有序的集合，我们定义它们的笛卡尔积 $\prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} X_i$（也记作 $\prod_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ 或 $X_1 \times ... \times X_n$）为

$$\prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} X_i := \{(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n} : x_i \in X_i \text{ 对所有 } 1 \leq i \leq n\}$$

同样地，这个定义只是假设当需要时有序 $n$ 元组和笛卡尔积总是存在的，但使用集合论公理可以显式地构造这些对象；参见习题 3.5.2。

**注 3.5.7** 可以将此构造推广到无限笛卡尔积；参见定义 8.4.1。

**例 3.5.8** 设 $a_1, b_1, a_2, b_2, a_3, b_3$ 是对象，并设 $X_1 := \{a_1, b_1\}$，$X_2 := \{a_2, b_2\}$，$X_3 := \{a_3, b_3\}$。则我们有

$$X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3 = \{(a_1, a_2, a_3), (a_1, a_2, b_3), (a_1, b_2, a_3), (a_1, b_2, b_3),$$
$$(b_1, a_2, a_3), (b_1, a_2, b_3), (b_1, b_2, a_3), (b_1, b_2, b_3)\}$$

$$(X_1 \times X_2) \times X_3 = \{((a_1, a_2), a_3), ((a_1, a_2), b_3), ((a_1, b_2), a_3), ((a_1, b_2), b_3),$$
$$((b_1, a_2), a_3), ((b_1, a_2), b_3), ((b_1, b_2), a_3), ((b_1, b_2), b_3)\}$$

$$X_1 \times (X_2 \times X_3) = \{(a_1, (a_2, a_3)), (a_1, (a_2, b_3)), (a_1, (b_2, a_3)), (a_1, (b_2, b_3)),$$
$$(b_1, (a_2, a_3)), (b_1, (a_2, b_3)), (b_1, (b_2, a_3)), (b_1, (b_2, b_3))\}$$

因此，严格来说，集合 $X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3$、$(X_1 \times X_2) \times X_3$ 和 $X_1 \times (X_2 \times X_3)$ 是不同的。然而，它们显然彼此密切相关（例如，任意两个集合之间存在明显的双射），在实践中通常忽略这些集合之间的细微区别，假设它们实际上是相等的。因此，一个函数 $f : X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3 \to Y$ 可以被认为是一个单变量 $(x_1, x_2, x_3) \in X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3$ 的函数，或者是三个变量 $x_1 \in X_1$、$x_2 \in X_2$、$x_3 \in X_3$ 的函数，或者是两个变量 $x_1 \in X_1$、$(x_2, x_3) \in X_2 \times X_3$ 的函数，等等；我们不区分这些不同的视角。

**注 3.5.9** 对象的有序 $n$ 元组 $(x_1, ..., x_n)$ 也称为长度为 $n$ 的有序序列，或简称有限序列。在第五章中我们还将介绍无限序列这一非常有用的概念。

**例 3.5.10** 如果 $x$ 是一个对象，则 $(x)$ 是一个 1 元组，我们将把它等同于 $x$ 本身（尽管严格来说它们不是同一个对象）。那么如果 $X_1$ 是任意集合，则笛卡尔积 $\prod_{1 \leq i \leq 1} X_i$ 就是 $X_1$（为什么？）。此外，空笛卡尔积 $\prod_{1 \leq i \leq 0} X_i$ 给出的不是空集 $\}$，而是单元素集 $\{\text{()}\}$，其唯一元素是 0 元组 $()$，也称为空元组。

如果 $n$ 是一个自然数，我们经常用 $X^n$ 作为 $n$ 重笛卡尔积 $X^n := \prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} X$ 的简写。因此 $X^1$ 本质上与集合 $X$ 相同（如果我们忽略对象 $x$ 和 1 元组 $(x)$ 之间的区别），而 $X^2$ 本质上就是笛卡尔积 $X \times X$。集合 $X^0$ 是一个单元素集 $\{\text{()}\}$（为什么？）。

我们现在可以将单选择引理（引理 3.1.5）推广到允许多个（但有限）选择的情况。

**引理 3.5.11**（有限选择）。设 $n \geq 1$ 是一个自然数，并对每个自然数 $1 \leq i \leq n$，设 $X_i$ 是一个非空集合。则存在一个有序 $n$ 元组 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 使得对所有 $1 \leq i \leq n$ 有 $x_i \in X_i$。换句话说，如果每个 $X_i$ 非空，则集合 $\prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} X_i$ 也是非空的。

**证明** 我们对 $n$ 进行归纳证明（从基例 $n = 1$ 开始；在 $n = 0$ 的情况下该结论也是平凡成立的，但在那种情况下并不特别有趣）。当 $n = 1$ 时，该结论由引理 3.1.5 得出（为什么？）。现在归纳地假设该结论对某个 $n$ 已经得到证明；我们现在证明它对 $n++$ 成立。设 $X_1, ..., X_{n++}$ 是一组非空集合。由归纳假设，我们可以找到一个有序 $n$ 元组 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 使得对所有 $1 \leq i \leq n$ 有 $x_i \in X_i$。此外，由于 $X_{n++}$ 非空，由引理 3.1.5 我们可以找到一个对象 $a$ 使得 $a \in X_{n++}$。如果我们这样定义有序 $n++$ 元组 $(y_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n++}$：当 $1 \leq i \leq n$ 时设 $y_i := x_i$，当 $i = n++$ 时设 $y_i := a$，则显然对所有 $1 \leq i \leq n++$ 有 $y_i \in X_i$，从而完成归纳。$\square$

**注 3.5.12** 这个引理应该可以推广到允许无限多个选择，这是很直观的，但不能自动做到；这需要一个额外的公理——选择公理。参见 8.4 节。

**习题 3.5.1**

(i) 假设我们对任意对象 $x$ 和 $y$ 通过公式 $(x, y) := \{\{x\}, \{x, y\}\}$（因此使用公理 3.4 的多次应用）定义有序对 $(x, y)$。例如，$(1, 2)$ 是集合 $\{{1\}, \{1, 2\}\}$，$(2, 1)$ 是集合 $\{{2\}, \{2, 1\}\}$，$(1, 1)$ 是集合 $\{{1\}}$。证明这样的定义（称为有序对的 Kuratowski 定义）确实满足性质 (3.5)。

(ii) 假设我们改用替代定义 $(x, y) := \{x, \{x, y\}\}$ 定义有序对。证明这个定义（称为有序对的简短定义）也满足 (3.5)，因此也是一个可接受的有序对定义。（警告：这很棘手；需要正则公理，特别是习题 3.2.2。）

(iii) 证明无论有序对的定义如何，任意两个集合 $X, Y$ 的笛卡尔积 $X \times Y$ 仍然是一个集合。（提示：首先使用替换公理证明对任意 $x \in X$，$\{(x, y) : y \in Y\}$ 是一个集合，然后再应用并集公理。）

**习题 3.5.2** 假设我们$^7$ 将有序 $n$ 元组定义为一个满射函数 $x : \{i \in \mathbb{N} : 1 \leq i \leq n\} \to X$，其陪域是某个任意集合 $X$（因此允许不同的有序 $n$ 元组有不同的值域）；我们记 $x(i)$ 为 $x_i$，也将 $x$ 记作 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$。使用这个定义，验证我们有 $(x_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n} = (y_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 当且仅当对所有 $1 \leq i \leq n$ 有 $x_i = y_i$。同时，证明如果 $(X_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$ 是一组有序的集合，则定义 3.5.6 中定义的笛卡尔积确实是一个集合。（提示：使用习题 3.4.7 和说明公理。）

**习题 3.5.3** 证明有序对和有序 $n$ 元组的相等性定义与自反性、对称性和传递性公理是一致的，即如果假设这些公理对有序对 $(x, y)$ 的各个分量 $x, y$ 成立，则它们对该有序对本身也成立。

**习题 3.5.4** 设 $A, B, C$ 是集合。证明 $A \times (B \cup C) = (A \times B) \cup (A \times C)$，$A \times (B \cap C) = (A \times B) \cap (A \times C)$，以及 $A \times (B \setminus C) = (A \times B) \setminus (A \times C)$。（当然可以证明类似的恒等式，其中笛卡尔积左右因子的角色互换。）

**习题 3.5.5** 设 $A, B, C, D$ 是集合。证明 $(A \times B) \cap (C \times D) = (A \cap C) \times (B \cap D)$。$(A \times B) \cup (C \times D) = (A \cup C) \times (B \cup D)$ 是否成立？$(A \times B) \setminus (C \times D) = (A \setminus C) \times (B \setminus D)$ 是否成立？

**习题 3.5.6** 设 $A, B, C, D$ 是非空集合。证明 $A \times B \subseteq C \times D$ 当且仅当 $A \subseteq C$ 和 $B \subseteq D$，且 $A \times B = C \times D$ 当且仅当 $A = C$ 和 $B = D$。如果移除 $A, B, C, D$ 是非空的这些假设中的一些或全部，会发生什么？

**习题 3.5.7** 设 $X, Y$ 是集合，并设 $\pi_{X \times Y \to X} : X \times Y \to X$ 和 $\pi_{X \times Y \to Y} : X \times Y \to Y$ 是映射 $\pi_{X \times Y \to X}(x, y) := x$ 和 $\pi_{X \times Y \to Y}(x, y) := y$；这些映射称为 $X \times Y$ 上的坐标函数。证明对任意函数 $f : Z \to X$ 和 $g : Z \to Y$，存在唯一的函数 $h : Z \to X \times Y$ 使得 $\pi_{X \times Y \to X} \circ h = f$ 和 $\pi_{X \times Y \to Y} \circ h = g$。（将其与习题 3.3.8 的最后一部分以及习题 3.1.7 进行比较。）这个函数 $h$ 称为 $f$ 和 $g$ 的配对，记作 $h = (f, g)$。

**习题 3.5.8** 设 $X_1, ..., X_n$ 是集合。证明笛卡尔积 $\prod_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ 为空当且仅当至少有一个 $X_i$ 为空。

**习题 3.5.9** 假设 $I$ 和 $J$ 是两个集合，并对所有 $\alpha \in I$ 设 $A_\alpha$ 是一个集合，对所有 $\beta \in J$ 设 $B_\beta$ 是一个集合。证明 $(\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} A_\alpha) \cap (\bigcup_{\beta \in J} B_\beta) = \bigcup_{(\alpha, \beta) \in I \times J} (A_\alpha \cap B_\beta)$。如果将这里所有的并集和交集符号互换，会发生什么？

**习题 3.5.10** 如果 $f : X \to Y$ 是一个函数，定义 $f$ 的图像为由 $\{(x, f(x)) : x \in X\}$ 定义的那些 $X \times Y$ 的子集。

(i) 证明两个函数 $f : X \to Y$, $\tilde{f} : X \to Y$ 相等当且仅当它们有相同的图像。

(ii) 相反地，如果 $G$ 是 $X \times Y$ 的任意子集且满足对每个 $x \in X$，集合 $\{y \in Y : (x, y) \in G\}$ 恰好有一个元素（换句话说，$G$ 满足垂直线测试），证明恰好存在一个函数 $f : X \to Y$ 使得其图像恰好是 $G$。

(iii) 假设我们定义$^8$ 一个函数 $f$ 为有序三元组 $f = (X, Y, G)$，其中 $X, Y$ 是集合，$G$ 是 $X \times Y$ 的一个满足垂直线测试的子集。我们随后定义该三元组的定义域为 $X$，陪域为 $Y$，并对每个 $x \in X$，定义 $f(x)$ 为满足 $(x, y) \in G$ 的唯一 $y \in Y$。证明这个定义与定义 3.3.1 是相容

sections.content_en:
and furthermore that this function is unique. (Hint: first show inductively, by a modification

of the proof of Lemma 3.5.11, that for every natural number N \inN, there exists a unique

function aN : {n \inN : n \leqN} \toX such that aN(0) = c and aN(n++) = f (n, aN(n)) for

all n \inN such that n < N.)

(ii) (Warning: this is challenging.) Prove (i) without using any properties of the natural numbers

other than the Peano axioms directly (in particular, without using the ordering of the natural

numbers, and without appealing to Proposition 2.1.16). (Hint: first show inductively, using

only the Peano axioms and basic set theory, that for every natural number N \inN, there exists

a unique pair AN, BN of subsets of N which obeys the following properties: (a) AN \capBN = \emptyset,

(b) AN \cupBN = N, (c) 0 \inAN, (d) N++ \inBN, (e) Whenever n \inBN, we have n++ \inBN.

(f) Whenever n \inAN and n ̸= N, we have n++ \inAN. Once one obtains these sets, use AN

as a substitute for {n \inN : n \leqN} in the previous argument.)

Exercise 3.5.13 The purpose of this exercise is to show that there is essentially only one version

of the natural number system in set theory (cf. the discussion in Remark 2.1.12). Suppose we have

a set N′ of “alternative natural numbers”, an “alternative zero” 0′, and an “alternative increment

operation” which takes any alternative natural number n′ \inN′ and returns another alternative natural

number n′++′ \inN′, such that the Peano axioms (Axioms 2.1-2.5) all hold with the natural numbers,

zero, and increment replaced by their alternative counterparts. Show that there exists a bijection

f : N \toN′ from the natural numbers to the alternative natural numbers such that f (0) = 0′, and

such that for any n \inN and n′ \inN′, we have f (n) = n′ if and only if f (n++) = n′++′. (Hint:

use Exercise 3.5.12.)

Cardinality of Sets

In the previous chapter we defined the natural numbers axiomatically, assuming

that they were equipped with a 0 and an increment operation, and assuming five

axioms on these numbers. Philosophically, this is quite different from one of our main

conceptualizations of natural numbers—that of cardinality, or measuring how many

elements there are in a set. Indeed, the Peano axiom approach treats natural numbers

more like ordinals than cardinals. (The cardinals are One, Two, Three, ..., and are

used to count how many things there are in a set. The ordinals are First, Second,

Third, ..., and are used to order a sequence of objects. There is a subtle difference

between the two, especially when comparing infinite cardinals with infinite ordinals,

but this is beyond the scope of this text.) We paid a lot of attention to what number

came next after a given number n—which is an operation which is quite natural for

ordinals, but less so for cardinals—but did not address the issue of whether these

numbers could be used to count sets. The purpose of this section is to address this

issue by noting that the natural numbers can be used to count the cardinality of sets,

as long as the set is finite.

The first thing is to work out when two sets have the same size. For instance, it

seems clear that the sets {1, 2, 3} and {4, 5, 6} have the same size, but that both have

a different size from {8, 9}. As an initial attempt to define a notion of size, we could

try to say that two sets have the same size if they have the same number of elements,

but we have not yet defined what the “number of elements” in a set is. Besides, this

runs into problems when a set is infinite.

Set Theory

The right way to define the concept of “two sets having the same size” is not

immediately obvious, but can be worked out with some thought. One intuitive reason why the sets {1, 2, 3} and {4, 5, 6} have the same size is that one can match the

elements of the first set with the elements in the second set in a one-to-one correspondence: 1 ↔4, 2 ↔5, 3 ↔6. (Indeed, this is how we first learn to count a set: we

correspond the set we are trying to count with another set, such as a set of fingers on

your hand.) We will use this intuitive understanding as our rigorous basis for “having

the same size”.

Definition 3.6.1 (Equal cardinality) We say that two sets X and Y have equal cardinality iff there exists a bijection f : X \toY from X to Y.

Example 3.6.2 The sets {0, 1, 2} and {3, 4, 5} have equal cardinality, since we can

find a bijection between the two sets. Note that we do not yet know whether {0, 1, 2}

and {3, 4} have equal cardinality; we know that one of the functions f from {0, 1, 2}

to {3, 4} is not a bijection, but we have not proven yet that there might still be some

other bijection from one set to the other. (It turns out that they do not have equal

cardinality, but we will prove this a little later.) Note that this definition makes sense

regardless of whether X is finite or infinite (in fact, we haven’t even defined what

finite means yet).

Remark 3.6.3 The fact that two sets have equal cardinality does not preclude one of

the sets from containing the other. For instance, if X is the set of natural numbers and

Y is the set of even9 natural numbers, then the map f : X \toY defined by f (n) := 2n

is a bijection from X to Y (why?), and so X and Y have equal cardinality, despite Y

being a subset of X and seeming intuitively as if it should only have “half” of the

elements of X.

The notion of having equal cardinality is an equivalence relation:

Proposition 3.6.4 Let X, Y, Z be sets. Then X has equal cardinality with X. If X

has equal cardinality with Y, then Y has equal cardinality with X. If X has equal

cardinality with Y and Y has equal cardinality with Z, then X has equal cardinality

with Z.

Proof See Exercise 3.6.1.

□

Let n be a natural number. Now we want to say when a set X has n elements.

Certainly we want the set {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn} = {1, 2, ... , n} to have n elements.

(This is true even when n = 0; the set {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leq0} is just the empty set.)

Using our notion of equal cardinality, we thus define:

Definition 3.6.5 Let n be a natural number. A set X is said to have cardinality n, iff

it has equal cardinality with {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn}. We also say that X has n elements

iff it has cardinality n.

9 A natural number is even if it is of the form 2n for some natural number n.

Remark 3.6.6 One can use the set {i \inN : i < n} instead of {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn},

since these two sets clearly have equal cardinality. (Why? What is the bijection?)

Example 3.6.7 Let a, b, c, d be distinct objects. Then {a, b, c, d} has the same cardinality as {i \inN : i < 4} = {0, 1, 2, 3} or {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leq4} = {1, 2, 3, 4} and thus

has cardinality 4. Similarly, the set {a} has cardinality 1.

There might be one problem with this definition: a set might have two different

cardinalities. But this is not possible:

Proposition 3.6.8 (Uniqueness of cardinality) Let X be a set with some cardinality

n. Then X cannot have any other cardinality, i.e., X cannot have cardinality m for

any m ̸= n.

Before we prove this proposition, we need a lemma.

Lemma 3.6.9 Suppose that n \geq1, and X has cardinality n. Then X is non-empty,

and if x is any element of X, then the set X −{x} (i.e., X with the element x removed)

has cardinality10n −1.

Proof If X is empty then it clearly cannot have the same cardinality as the nonempty set {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn}, as there is no bijection from the empty set to a nonempty set (why?). Now let x be an element of X. Since X has the same cardinality

as {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn}, we thus have a bijection f from X to {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn}.

In particular, f (x) is a natural number between 1 and n. Now define the function

g : X −{x} \to{i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn −1} by the following rule: for any y \inX −{x},

we define g(y) := f (y) if f (y) < f (x), and define g(y) := f (y) −1 if f (y) >

f (x). (Note that f (y) cannot equal f (x) since y ̸= x and f is a bijection.) It is easy

to check that this map is also a bijection (why?), and so X −{x} has equal cardinality with {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn −1}. In particular X −{x} has cardinality n −1, as

desired.

□

Now we prove the proposition.

Proof of Proposition 3.6.8 We induct on n. First suppose that n = 0. Then X must

be empty, and so X cannot have any non-zero cardinality. Now suppose that the

proposition is already proven for some n; we now prove it for n++. Let X have

cardinality n++; and suppose that X also has some other cardinality m ̸= n++.

By Lemma 3.6.9, X is non-empty, and if x is any element of X, then X −{x}

has cardinality n and also has cardinality m −1, by Lemma 3.6.9. By induction

hypothesis,thismeansthatn = m −1,whichimpliesthatm = n++,acontradiction.

This closes the induction.

□

10 Strictly speaking, n −1 has not yet been defined in this text. For the purposes of this lemma, we

define n −1 to be the unique natural number m such that m++ = n; this m is given by Lemma

2.2.10.

Set Theory

Thus, for instance, we now know, thanks to Propositions 3.6.4 and 3.6.8, that the

sets {0, 1, 2} and {3, 4} do not have equal cardinality, since the first set has cardinality

3 and the second set has cardinality 2.

Definition 3.6.10 (Finite sets). A set is finite iff it has cardinality n for some natural

number n; otherwise, the set is called infinite. If X is a finite set, we use #(X) to

denote the cardinality of X.

Example 3.6.11 The sets {0, 1, 2} and {3, 4} are finite, as is the empty set (0 is a

natural number), and #({0, 1, 2}) = 3, #({3, 4}) = 2, and #(\emptyset) = 0.

Now we give an example of an infinite set.

Theorem 3.6.12 The set of natural numbers N is infinite.

Proof Suppose for sake of contradiction that the set of natural numbers N was

finite, so it had some cardinality #(N) = n. By Lemma 3.6.9, N\{0} would then have

cardinality n −1. But N has equal cardinality with N\{0} (using x  \tox + 1 as the

bijection from the latter to the former), hence n = n −1, which gives the desired

contradiction.

□

Remark 3.6.13 One can also use similar arguments to show that any unbounded

set11 is infinite; for instance the rationals Q and the reals R (which we will construct

in later chapters) are infinite. However, it is possible for some sets to be “more”

infinite than others; see Sect. 8.3.

Now we relate cardinality with the arithmetic of natural numbers.

Proposition 3.6.14 (Cardinal arithmetic).

(a) Let X be a finite set, and let x be an object which is not an element of X. Then

X \cup{x} is finite and #(X \cup{x}) = #(X) + 1.

(b) Let X and Y be finite sets. Then X \cupY is finite and #(X \cupY) \leq#(X) + #(Y).

If in addition X and Y are disjoint (i.e., X \capY = \emptyset), then #(X \cupY) = #(X) +

#(Y).

(c) Let X be a finite set, and let Y be a subset of X. Then Y is finite, and #(Y) \leq#(X).

If in addition Y ̸= X (i.e., Y is a proper subset of X), then we have #(Y) < #(X).

(d) If X is a finite set, and f : X \toY is a function, then f (X) is a finite set with

#( f (X)) \leq#(X). One has equality #( f (X)) = #(X) if and only if f is one-toone.

(e) Let X and Y be finite sets. Then Cartesian product X \times Y is finite and #(X \times

Y) = #(X) \times #(Y).

(f ) Let X and Y be finite sets. Then the set Y X (defined in Axiom 3.11) is finite and

#(Y X) = #(Y)#(X).

Proof See Exercise 3.6.4.

□

11 The notion of a bounded or unbounded set is defined in Definition 9.1.22.

Remark 3.6.15 Proposition 3.6.14 suggests that there is another way to define the

arithmetic operations of natural numbers; not defined recursively as in Definitions

2.2.1, 2.3.1, 2.3.11, but instead using the notions of union, Cartesian product, and

power set. This is the basis of cardinal arithmetic, which is an alternative foundation

to arithmetic than the Peano arithmetic we have developed here; we will not develop

this arithmetic in this text, but we give some examples of how one would work with

this arithmetic in Exercises 3.6.5, 3.6.6.

This concludes our discussion of finite sets. We shall discuss infinite sets in Chap.

8, once we have constructed a few more examples of infinite sets (such as the integers,

rationals, and reals).

Exercise 3.6.1 Prove Proposition 3.6.4.

Exercise 3.6.2 Show that a set X has cardinality 0 if and only if X is the empty set.

Exercise 3.6.3 Let n be a natural number, and let f : {i \inN : 1 \leqi \leqn} \toN be a function. Show

that there exists a natural number M such that f (i) \leqM for all 1 \leqi \leqn. (Hint: induct on n. You

may also want to peek at Lemma 5.1.14.) Thus finite subsets of the natural numbers are bounded.

Use this to give an alternate proof of Theorem 3.6.12 that does not use Lemma 3.6.9.

Exercise 3.6.4 Prove Proposition 3.6.14.

Exercise 3.6.5 Let A and B be sets. Show that A \times B and B \times A have equal cardinality by constructing an explicit bijection between the two sets. Then use Proposition 3.6.14 to conclude an

alternate proof of Lemma 2.3.2.

Exercise 3.6.6 Let A, B, C be sets. Show that the sets (AB)C and AB\timesC have equal cardinality by

constructing an explicit bijection between the two sets. Conclude that (ab)c = abc for any natural

numbers a, b, c. Use a similar argument to also conclude ab \times ac = ab+c.

Exercise 3.6.7 Let A and B be sets. Let us say that A has lesser or equal cardinality to B if there

exists an injection f : A \toB from A to B. Show that if A and B are finite sets, then A has lesser

or equal cardinality to B if and only if #(A) \leq#(B).

Exercise 3.6.8 Let A and B be sets such that there exists an injection f : A \toB from A to B (i.e.,

A has lesser or equal cardinality to B). Assume also that A is non-empty. Show that there exists a

surjection g: B \toA from B to A. (The converse to this statement requires the axiom of choice;

see Exercise 8.4.3.)

Exercise 3.6.9 Let A and B be finite sets. Show that A \cupB and A \capB are also finite sets, and that

#(A) + #(B) = #(A \cupB) + #(A \capB).

Exercise 3.6.10 Let A1, ... , An be finite sets such that #(

i\in{1,...,n} Ai) > n. Show that there

exists i \in{1, ... , n} such that #(Ai) \geq2. (This is known as the pigeonhole principle.)

Exercise 3.6.11 Let f : X \toY be a function between two sets X, Y. Show that the following are

equivalent:

(a)

f is injective.

(b) Whenever E \subseteqX has cardinality #(E) equal to 2, then the image f (E) also has cardinality

#( f (E)) = 2.
